back: An abstract data type where data elements are arranged in a sequential order, and each member element is connected to its previous and next element in a single level. Examples include stacks and queues.
A linear data structure that follows a particular order in which operations are performed. The order is Last In First Out (LIFO), meaning the element added last is the first one to be removed.
In the context of queues, the start of the queue where the first element resides and from which elements are removed during the dequeue operation.
In the context of queues, the end of the queue where new elements are added during the enqueue operation.
In the context of stacks, the last added element and the current endpoint of the structure. It is the first element to be removed due to the LIFO policy.
In the context of stacks, the bottom of the stack where the first inserted element resides.
A principle used primarily in stacks where the last element added to the structure is the first one to be removed.
A notation in which operators are written in-between their operands, such as the expression A + B.
A notation in which operators precede their operands. For example, the infix expression A + B would be written as +AB.
A notation in which operators follow their operands. For example, the infix expression A + B would be written as AB+.
An expression where every binary or unary operation is explicitly enclosed in parentheses to specify the order of operations without ambiguity.
A linear data structure that follows the First In First Out (FIFO) principle, where the first element added is the first one to be removed.
A principle used primarily in queues where the first element added to the structure is the first one to be removed.
Short for double-ended queue, it is a data structure that allows the insertion and removal of elements from both the front and the rear, providing more flexibility than standard queues or stacks.
